author: @danedmiston9673
text: This should be a great project!!!  Now you can start comparing turbine designs for power.  Usually the best power is achieved when the prony brake has slowed the turbine to about one half the free running RPM.  (300 RPM  X  0 torque  =  0 power  and  0 RPM  X  high torque also = 0 power  ----  somewhere near the middle RPM = best power

author: @lukedominicodonnell2347
text: Your illustration made no visual sense! The counterweight and the weight look as though they weigh down the rod.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: The illustration is confusing because the rotor is turning the wrong way.  The counter weight is there to balance and center the rod between the two stops when there is no drag on the shaft.  As the brake is tighten on the [counterclockwise] turning shaft. The drag will lift the rod off center and hit the top stop.  Weight is added to the rod until it is centered again.  The length between the center of the shaft and the weight, multiplied by the weight can then be converted to foot pounds of torque.  Then the torque times the RPMs of the shaft will equal the power being taken from the shaft.

author: @wizrom3046
text:
You also need an accurate RPM meter, because;   power = torque × RPM
if you don't already have one, ebay sell little handheld optical RPM meters, you just point it at a spinning object and it tells you the RPM, very handy.
Looking forward to seeing your setup.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - thanks for the tip - cheers

author: @bolamnieplecy6363
text: Great animation thanks

author: @bradarmstrong3952
text: Wouldn't the force required to stop the spinning shaft vary depending on the coefficient of friction at the interface of the material the braking is made of and the shaft?

replies.author: @wizrom3046
replies.text:
Hi, you don't stop the shaft because zero power is generated by a stopped shaft.
Instead, you slow the shaft to different RPM speed values, and measure the brake torque at each speed.
Then you chart the result as   torque × RPM   and get a dyno chart. At some particular RPM speed on the dyno chart you will see the maximum power and what speed it occurs.

replies.author: @bradarmstrong3952
replies.text: @wizrom3046 that makes perfect sense, but in the video he said that you slow the shaft down until you stop it and that tells you the power

replies.author: @wizrom3046

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: Can you put a motor/ generator on each end?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would think - but then it would be a derived measure wouldn't it?

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Good point.  Always pros and cons.   Complexity which we all hate, Cost and time is a con also. 
 Pros?   Once proof of concept achieved, it adds redundancy reducing risk if one motor fails.   2.  Motors are designed for different torq vs rpms, so one can be optimized for lower wind, while the other for higher winds producing as high as 10x more power but it all depends.  Anyways, pros and cons.  :)
